title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant stream flowing through our rohe that holds cultural, historical and ecological importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native tree ferns that once dominated its banks and its role as a traditional food gathering place.
learningObjectives: Analyze historical and contemporary human impacts on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using primary source evidence
Apply mātauranga Māori principles of kaitiakitanga to develop sustainable management strategies for the awa
Collaborate with local kaitiaki to document and present findings on stream health and restoration opportunities
Evaluate the effectiveness of different community-led conservation approaches for urban waterways

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua to hear pūrākau (traditional stories), learn traditional place names, and understand the historical significance of the waterway as a mahinga kai (food gathering place) and transport route

activities.title: Taiao Investigation - Stream Health Monitoring
activities.description: Conduct water quality testing using scientific equipment and mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of native species, water clarity, riparian vegetation health) at three different points along the stream

activities.title: Historical Timeline of Human Impact
activities.description: Research and create a digital timeline showing how land use changes (from pre-European settlement to modern urban development) have affected the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku

activities.title: Kaitiaki Interviews
activities.description: Interview local environmental kaitiaki from Ngāti Kahungunu and Pākehā conservation groups about their restoration efforts, challenges, and visions for the future of the awa

activities.title: Sustainable Solutions Wānanga
activities.description: Design and present culturally-responsive restoration proposals that blend modern science with mātauranga Māori, addressing specific issues identified during the investigation

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring data and GIS mapping tools
Local kaitiaki from Manaaki Te Awa Trust and Ngāti Kahungunu environmental unit
Traditional ecological knowledge resources from Te Papa Tongarewa digital collections

tasks: Produce a comprehensive stream health report with water quality data analysis
Create a digital storymap documenting historical changes and cultural significance
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Write a reflective journal on personal connections to place and responsibility as kaitiaki

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices and values contribute to sustainable communities
